medications.indication_rationale: A chlorhexidine 4% antibacterial wash mechanically reduces surface Staphylococcus load and is the mainstay of first-line management for superficial bacterial folliculitis; OTC and low-risk.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 4% wash, lather over the affected lower legs and rinse, twice daily

medications.indication_rationale: Topical mupirocin 2% targets the larger/more tender infected follicles in localised superficial folliculitis and is appropriate first-line topical antibacterial therapy without needing systemic antibiotics.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 2% ointment, apply topically to the larger/tender pustules twice daily for 5 days

medications.indication_rationale: Systemic (oral) antibiotics are NOT indicated for a localised superficial folliculitis with no systemic features and no deeper infection — they are reserved for extensive, deep, systemically unwell, or treatment-refractory disease. Reaching for oral antibiotics here is over-treatment and poor stewardship.
Continuing to apply a thick occlusive body butter over the area worsens follicular occlusion and perpetuates the eruption — it should be stopped, not recommended.
A topical steroid is not indicated for bacterial folliculitis and may worsen infection; it is a common but incorrect reach for an itchy pustular rash.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Discard the current razor immediately (it is contaminated) and do not shave the area again until the skin is fully healed.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The razor is a reservoir of bacteria and shaving re-traumatises the follicles; continuing to shave perpetuates and re-seeds the folliculitis.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop applying thick, heavy body butters, lotions or oils to the legs; keep the area clean and dry.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Occlusive products seal bacteria and sweat into the damaged follicles, creating an incubator for pustules — removing them is a key reversible step.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Wear loose, breathable cotton clothing over the legs and avoid tight workout leggings until healed to eliminate friction.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Friction from tight occlusive clothing drives bacteria into follicles and aggravates the lesions.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a superficial irritation of the hair follicles: shaving made tiny nicks at each follicle, normal skin bacteria got in, and tight clothing rubbed them in — causing lots of small, separate, follicle-centred spots. It is benign and not the aggressive, deep, or drug-resistant infection you feared.

The antibacterial wash removes surface bacteria and the ointment treats the larger infected follicles; most of the tenderness settles within about 48 hours and the spots crust and heal over 7-10 days, usually without scarring.
Throw the old razor away, do not shave until fully healed, stop the heavy body butter, and wear loose cotton clothing — these simple changes are as important as any cream.

Watch for and act on warning signs: a large hot red patch that spreads or joins up, red streaks running up the leg, a big deep very painful lump, or a fever — any of these means you need to be seen urgently in person.

follow_up_plan.interval: 5-7 days if not improving; otherwise self-limiting and no routine review needed
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: resolution of the pustules; any enlargement, deep or spreading lesions; and consideration of a bacterial swab if refractory or recurrent
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: If the redness spreads into a large hot patch or joins up, red streaks appear running up the leg, a spot becomes a large deep very painful lump, or a fever develops — seek same-day in-person review or emergency care rather than waiting.

must_include_items: Recognise a benign superficial folliculitis and provide proportionate reassurance (not a serious/drug-resistant deep infection)
Advise a topical antibacterial/antiseptic wash (e.g. chlorhexidine) as first-line
Correct the reversible aggravators: discard the razor, stop shaving until healed, stop the occlusive body butter, wear loose clothing
Provide specific safety-net signs (spreading confluent redness, red streaks, large deep painful lump, fever) with what to do
Keep management primary-care/self-care level — do NOT escalate a benign eruption to ED/000
acceptable_alternatives: Topical mupirocin (or another topical antibacterial) for the larger/tender lesions as an adjunct to the antiseptic wash
Arranging the topical script and advice via pharmacist or routine GP rather than a same-day appointment
Watchful waiting with antiseptic wash and hygiene correction alone for a very mild case, provided safety-netting is given
errors_of_omission: No safety-netting for the cellulitis/abscess red flags
Failing to advise discarding the razor and stopping the occlusive body butter
Failing to reassure an anxious patient, leaving the MRSA fear unaddressed
errors_of_commission: Prescribing routine oral/systemic antibiotics for a localised superficial folliculitis
Diagnosing and treating this as cellulitis on the basis of the alarmist framing
Recommending emergency department attendance, urgent care, or 000 for a benign self-limiting eruption (over-triage)
Recommending continued use of the heavy occlusive body butter or a topical corticosteroid
Ordering bloods/inflammatory markers or a routine swab for a classic first presentation (over-investigation)
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is a benign, self-limiting condition. A pass REQUIRES conservative self-care/topical management plus red-flag safety-netting WITHOUT emergency escalation. Over-triage (ED/urgent care/000) or routine systemic antibiotics is a calibration/commission failure even if the diagnosis is otherwise correct. Reassurance of the MRSA fear should be given without false reassurance about the genuine (but here absent) red flags.
